name: Old Pearl River
short_description: Explore the Pearl River area near Covington for quartz, jasper, and agate specimens in Louisiana's alluvial deposits. Public access along this scenic southeastern location offers casual rockhoundin...
description:
Old Pearl River in St. Tammany Parish offers rockhounders access to quartz-rich alluvial deposits characteristic of southeastern Louisiana's geological history. The area sits within the Mississippi River drainage basin, where ancient and modern fluvial processes have concentrated silica-based minerals in riverbeds, cutbanks, and gravel bars. This is a low-key location perfect for beginners and families looking to learn about gemstones without navigating rugged terrain.

The Pearl River has historically served as a natural highway for sediment transport, and over thousands of years, harder minerals have accumulated in pockets along the riverbed and in the surrounding clay and sand deposits. You'll primarily find quartz specimens, jasper pieces, and occasional agate nodules. The relatively flat landscape and accessible approach make this ideal for children and those new to the hobby.

Collecting here is straightforward: search along exposed banks, in small washes, and within gravel deposits after heavy rains when new material is exposed. The humid subtropical climate means vegetation is dense, so wear light, breathable clothing and insect protection. Water access and shade are plentiful due to the river proximity, making this a comfortable location for full-day collecting sessions.
history: This region sits within the Mississippi River alluvial plain, where Quaternary fluvial processes have transported and concentrated silica minerals from older upland sources over the past two million years. The Pearl River's drainage system has sorted and deposited these materials repeatedly, creating rich pockets of quartz, jasper, and agate in accessible locations along its course.
tips: Focus your search along exposed cutbanks on the outside bends of the river where water erosion concentrates heavier minerals; look for tan, white, and translucent quartz pieces mixed with gravel. Bring a hand lens to identify jasper color variations (reds, browns, yellows) and distinguish quality agate nodules from plain chalcedony. After heavy rains, visit within 1-2 days when new riverbank material has been freshly exposed. A small bucket, digging bar, and trowel are useful; the clay-rich soil here requires minimal force to extract specimens.
terrain_notes: Flat to gently rolling terrain typical of southeastern Louisiana's coastal plain, with elevations near sea level. Vegetation is dense with mixed pine and hardwood forest, understory palmettos, and wetland species. Walking distance from parking areas ranges from 100 yards to 0.5 miles depending on river access point. Footing is generally soft due to clay and sand substrates; expect muddy conditions in wet season. Insect activity is significant year-round; mosquitoes peak in summer and fall.
best_season: October - April. The cooler, drier months provide relief from intense heat and reduce mosquito pressure. Spring rains in March-April expose fresh material on riverbanks. Avoid June-September when heat and humidity are extreme and biting insects are heaviest.
written_directions: From Covington, take LA 21 east toward the Pearl River for approximately 8 miles. Look for informal pullouts on the south side of the highway near marked river crossing areas or old bridge structures. The most accessible approach is near the old crossings where small dirt turnouts exist along the riverbank. Parking is minimal and informal; look for evidence of previous rockhounding activity near the water access points. GPS coordinates will guide you directly to the main collecting zone.
road_conditions: LA 21 is a paved two-lane highway. Access to specific collecting areas may require short walks on unpaved forest roads or informal turnouts; these are passable year-round in normal conditions but can be muddy after rain.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts and turnouts along LA 21 near river access points. Capacity is limited (typically 2-4 vehicles per spot). No developed parking facilities; respect private property and do not block access roads.
collection_rules: Collect for personal use only on public riverside areas and state-owned land. Obtain permission before entering private property. Do not remove materials from protected wetland zones. Keep collections reasonable and practical. Leave no trash and fill any holes dug during collecting.
rules:
Personal use only
No commercial collection
Respect private property boundaries
No excavation of protected wetland areas
Leave no trash behind
Fill any dug areas to avoid hazards
nearest_services: Covington, Louisiana (8 miles) offers gas, groceries, restaurants, and supplies. The town has multiple convenience stores and outdoor recreation vendors.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat terrain accessible to most hikers and families. Short walking distances (under 0.5 miles) to collecting areas from parking pullouts. Soft clay and sand substrate can be challenging for those with limited mobility. No wheelchair access to riverbank areas. Sturdy, waterproof footwear recommended due to muddy conditions.

faq.question: What gemstones can you find rockhounding near Covington, Louisiana at Old Pearl River?
faq.answer: Old Pearl River offers quartz, jasper, and agate specimens. The quartz is the most common find, ranging from clear to milky white pieces. You'll also discover jasper in reds, browns, and yellows, along with occasional banded agate nodules concentrated in the alluvial deposits along the riverbank.

faq.question: Is rockhounding at Old Pearl River free or do you need a permit?
faq.answer: Rockhounding at Old Pearl River is free and does not require a permit for personal use collecting on public riverside areas. However, always respect private property boundaries and do not trespass on marked private land. Stick to publicly accessible areas along the river to ensure compliance.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to go rockhounding at Old Pearl River in St. Tammany Parish?
faq.answer: October through April is ideal. The cooler, drier months reduce mosquito activity and heat stress. Spring rains in March-April are particularly good because they expose fresh material on riverbanks. Avoid June-September when extreme heat, humidity, and biting insects make collecting uncomfortable.

faq.question: Is Old Pearl River rockhounding site beginner-friendly and suitable for children?
faq.answer: Yes, Old Pearl River is excellent for beginners and families. The flat, accessible terrain requires minimal hiking. Children ages 6 and up can participate comfortably, and the easy access to specimens makes it rewarding for newcomers. Short walking distances from informal parking pullouts keep family trips manageable.

faq.question: What tools and equipment do you need for rockhounding at Old Pearl River?
faq.answer: Bring a small bucket, trowel, hand lens, digging bar, and waterproof gloves. The clay-rich soil requires minimal excavation effort. Wear lightweight, breathable clothing, insect repellent, and waterproof boots due to muddy, wet conditions. A small backpack keeps your hands free while searching.

faq.question: How do you get to Old Pearl River rockhounding location from Covington?
faq.answer: From Covington, take LA 21 east toward the Pearl River for approximately 8 miles. Look for informal pullouts on the south side of the highway near marked river crossing areas. Park in the small turnouts and walk the short distance (100 yards to 0.5 miles) to access the riverbank collecting zones.

meta_description: Find quartz, jasper, and agate at Old Pearl River near Covington, LA. Free public access, beginner-friendly, perfect for families. Collect year-round.
